key_facts: GPCR is a seven-pass transmembrane protein
>700 members in humans
~400 functional olfactory receptor genes
~30% of drugs work through GPCRs
Examples: rhodopsin, olfactory receptors, adrenaline receptors
common_confusions: GPCR (7-pass) vs RTK (single-pass) transmembrane topology
Confusing GPCR with ion-channel-coupled receptors
exam_traps: Nobel Prize 2012 to Lefkowitz for GPCR research

key_facts: Heterotrimeric G protein has alpha, beta, gamma subunits
beta and gamma tethered to plasma membrane by lipid tails
alpha subunit: GDP-bound = inactive; GTP-bound = active
Ligand binding -> GDP dissociates -> GTP binds -> Galpha-GTP + Gbetagamma both become active
Signal turned off by intrinsic GTPase activity of Galpha
>20 types of G proteins in mammals
common_confusions: Thinking only Galpha is active after dissociation — Gbetagamma is also active
Thinking signal duration is controlled by phosphatases — it is controlled by Galpha GTPase activity
numeric_values: >20 types of G proteins in mammals
exam_traps: Both Galpha AND Gbetagamma activate effectors
Signal duration = Galpha GTPase activity (answer d in the practice MCQ)

key_facts: Three major effectors: ion channels, adenylyl cyclase, phospholipase C
Gbetagamma opens K+ channels in heart pacemaker cells -> slows heart rate
Adenylyl cyclase activated by Galpha of Gs -> ATP -> cAMP
PLC activated -> PIP2 -> IP3 + DAG
common_confusions: Mixing up which G protein subunit activates which effector
Forgetting that Gbetagamma (not just Galpha) opens K+ channels
exam_traps: Gbetagamma opens K+ channel in heart — it is Gbetagamma not Galpha

key_facts: Adrenaline -> adrenergic GPCR -> Gs -> adenylyl cyclase -> ATP -> cAMP
cAMP activates PKA
PKA effects: increase heart rate, glycogen breakdown in muscle, gene transcription in neurons (long-term memory)
common_confusions: cAMP activates PKA not PKC
Mixing up PKA (cAMP pathway) with PKC (DAG+Ca2+ pathway)
exam_traps: cAMP -> PKA (not PKC); DAG + Ca2+ -> PKC

key_facts: PLC cleaves PIP2 -> IP3 + DAG
IP3 opens Ca2+ channels on ER membrane -> Ca2+ release into cytosol
Ca2+ steady state: ER ~1 mM; cytosol ~10^-4 mM
DAG stays in membrane, activates PKC together with Ca2+
Fertilization of egg by sperm triggers Ca2+ wave
common_confusions: Naming only IP3 and forgetting DAG, or vice versa
Thinking Ca2+ comes from outside the cell — initial source is ER
numeric_values: Ca2+ in cytosol at steady state: ~10^-4 mM
exam_traps: PLC makes TWO second messengers: IP3 AND DAG
Ca2+ source = ER (not extracellular) for the initial signal

key_facts: Single-pass transmembrane proteins
Ligand (usually dimer) causes receptor dimerization
Respond to growth factors regulating growth, proliferation, differentiation, survival
Responses lead to gene expression changes
Major role in cancer development
common_confusions: Confusing single-pass (RTK) with seven-pass (GPCR)
exam_traps: Enzyme-coupled receptors are single-pass; GPCRs are 7-pass

key_facts: Three subtypes: tyrosine kinase receptors (RTKs), serine/threonine kinase receptors, guanylyl cyclase-linked receptors
RTKs are the major class relevant to cancer and metabolic disorders
common_confusions: Thinking there are only two subtypes of enzyme-coupled receptors
exam_traps: Three subtypes of enzyme-coupled receptors, not two

key_facts: Ligand binding causes RTK dimerization
Dimerization activates intracellular kinase domain
Kinase domain phosphorylates tyrosines on adjacent tail (transautophosphorylation)
Each phosphotyrosine = specific docking site for a different signaling protein
Multiple parallel pathways activated simultaneously
common_confusions: Thinking RTKs dimerize before ligand binding
Thinking all phosphotyrosines recruit the same signaling protein
exam_traps: Ligand induces dimerization; each phosphotyrosine recruits a DIFFERENT specific protein

key_facts: Mutate individual tyrosine to alanine (cannot be phosphorylated)
Loss of a specific signaling protein binding identifies which phosphotyrosine is its docking site
Overactive constitutively active Ras used to determine ordering of proteins in pathway
common_confusions: Confusing what conclusion can be drawn: absence of binding after Y to A mutation means that tyrosine is the docking site
exam_traps: Y to Alanine mutation: if protein X no longer binds, phospho-Y is the docking site for X

key_facts: RTK -> adaptor protein (Grb2) docks on phosphotyrosine -> recruits Ras-GEF (SOS)
Ras-GEF promotes GDP to GTP exchange on Ras
Active Ras-GTP -> Raf -> MEK -> MAPK -> altered gene expression
30% of human cancers have activating Ras mutations
Constitutively active Ras used to determine pathway order
common_confusions: Thinking Ras-GEF is a kinase — it promotes nucleotide exchange, not phosphorylation
Confusing Ras pathway with PI3K pathway
numeric_values: 30% of human cancers have activating Ras mutations
exam_traps: Ras-GEF = guanine nucleotide exchange factor, NOT a kinase
Ras turns off via its own GTPase activity (parallel to Galpha)

key_facts: RTK -> PI3-kinase -> phosphorylates inositol phospholipid -> PIP3
PIP3 recruits PDK1 and Akt to membrane
PDK1 activates Akt
Akt promotes cell growth and survival
PTEN dephosphorylates PIP3 -> opposes PI3K; PTEN is tumor suppressor
common_confusions: Thinking PI3-kinase phosphorylates a protein — it phosphorylates a lipid
Forgetting PTEN as the negative regulator
exam_traps: PI3-kinase substrate is a membrane LIPID not a protein; PIP3 is the lipid second messenger

key_facts: EGFR is an RTK activated by EGF
Activates both Ras/MAPK (via Grb2-SOS) and PI3K/AKT simultaneously
Downstream: survival, proliferation, invasion, metastasis, angiogenesis
PTEN opposes PI3K arm
common_confusions: Thinking EGFR activates only one pathway — it activates multiple simultaneously
exam_traps: EGFR simultaneously activates Ras/MAPK AND PI3K/Akt via different phosphotyrosines

key_facts: EGFR overexpressed in head/neck, ovary, cervix cancers
Many epithelial cancers have mutations constitutively activating EGFR
EGFRvIII = deletion mutant lacking extracellular domain; constitutively active
exam_traps: EGFRvIII is constitutively active even without ligand due to deletion of extracellular domain

key_facts: TKIs: block intracellular ATP-binding site of kinase domain
Anti-EGFR mAbs: block extracellular domain to prevent ligand binding or dimerization
Two distinct inhibition sites on the same receptor
common_confusions: Confusing TKI (intracellular) with mAb (extracellular) mechanisms
exam_traps: TKI = intracellular kinase domain; mAb = extracellular domain — different sites

key_facts: 1st/2nd gen TKIs (Gefitinib, Erlotinib, Afatinib) -> acquire T790M resistance mutation
3rd gen TKIs (AZD9291/Osimertinib, CO-1686, HM61713) overcome T790M -> acquire C797S resistance
4th gen TKI (EAI045) designed for C797S
T790M-positive patients have shorter progression-free survival than T790M-negative
common_confusions: Mixing up which generation targets which mutation
Thinking resistance mutations are pre-existing — they are acquired after treatment
numeric_values: T790M-positive vs T790M-negative: significant difference in PFS, p<0.001
exam_traps: Resistance mutations are ACQUIRED after treatment, not present at baseline in most patients
